title: Reliable Active Apprenticeship Learning
abstract: We propose a learning problem, which we call reliable active apprenticeship learning, for which we define a learning algorithm providing optimal performance guarantees, which we further show are sharply characterized by the eluder dimension of a policy class.  In this setting, a learning algorithm is tasked with behaving optimally in an unknown environment given by a Markov decision process.  The correct actions are specified by an unknown optimal policy in a given policy class.  The learner initially does not know the optimal policy, but it has the ability to query an expert, which returns the optimal action for the current state.  A learner is said to be reliable if, whenever it takes an action without querying the expert, its action is guaranteed to be optimal.  We are then interested in designing a reliable learner which does not query the expert too often.  We propose a reliable learning algorithm which provably makes the minimal possible number of queries, which we show is precisely characterized by the eluder dimension of the policy class.  We further extend this to allow for imperfect experts, modeled as an oracle with noisy responses.  We study two variants of this, inspired by noise conditions from classification: namely, Massart noise and Tsybakov noise.  In both cases, we propose a reliable learning strategy which achieves a nearly-minimal number of queries, and prove upper and lower bounds on the optimal number of queries in terms of the noise conditions and the eluder dimension of the policy class.
author: Steve Hanneke;Liu Yang;Gongju Wang;Yulun Song
aff: Purdue University;Data Intelligence Division, China Unicom Digital Technology Co., Ltd.;;
aff_unique_norm: Purdue University;Data Intelligence Division, China Unicom Digital Technology Co., Ltd.;

roast.laymen_summary: The authors created an AI intern that is so terrified of failing it refuses to move unless it’s 100% sure it’s right, otherwise it pesteringly asks a human expert what to do. The paper basically tries to calculate exactly how many times this annoying bot will blow up your Slack notifications before it finally learns to work independently.
why_bad: The 'reliability' requirement is a total buzzkill; in the real world, an agent that can't make a single mistake basically just stands in the corner until the expert does the whole job for it.
Basing the entire proof on 'eluder dimension' is a classic academic flex—it sounds sophisticated, but it's notoriously impossible to calculate for any AI model people actually use.

Applying Massart and Tsybakov noise to reinforcement learning feels like trying to fix a SpaceX rocket using a 1990s Honda Civic manual; the theoretical assumptions just don't survive the transition to complex environments.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a startup where we've 'disrupted' the onboarding process by hiring an intern who is legally prohibited from making a typo. Instead of 'moving fast and breaking things,' this intern stands behind your desk and asks, 'Should I press the Enter key now?' for every single line of code. Our 'groundbreaking' algorithm predicts exactly how many times you’ll want to throw your monitor out the window based on how confusing your job is (the Eluder Dimension). We even factored in what happens if you, the boss, are caffeinated and occasionally give wrong directions. It’s not an autonomous worker; it’s a high-tech way to ensure you never get your own work done while 'teaching' a bot that’s too scared to live.

title: Center-Based Approximation of a Drifting Distribution
abstract: We present a novel technique for computing a center-based approximation of a drifting distribution. Given $k \geq 1$ and a sequence of data, whose distribution is changing over time, the goal is to compute, at each step, the best $k$ centers representation of the current distribution, despite possibly having only a single sample from the most recent distribution. In data mining, this is traditionally attempted through the sliding-window mechanism, where the analysis is performed on the most recent fixed-size segment of the data. The problems with this approach are twofold: (1) setting the correct window size is challenging; and (2) a fixed window size cannot effectively track changes in the distribution happening at variable speed. In this paper, we propose a new methodology that dynamically adjusts the window size based on the recent drift of the data. The challenge is that it is not possible to explicitly estimate the drift, as we may have only a single data point from each distribution. Our main contribution lies in providing a rigorous mathematical analysis, establishing both an upper bound via a dynamic window size algorithm, and a lower bound that shows the tightness of our approach.
author: Alessio Mazzetto;Matteo Ceccarello;Andrea Pietracaprina;Geppino Pucci;Eli Upfal
aff: Brown University;University of Padua;University of Padua;University of Padua;Brown University
position: PhD student;Assistant Professor;Full Professor;Full Professor;Full Professor

roast.laymen_summary: The authors are trying to find the 'middle' of a data group that won't stop moving, using a window that expands and shrinks like a nervous accordion. It’s essentially a high-math attempt to play 'Where’s Waldo' when Waldo is sprinting and you’re only allowed to look through a straw.
why_bad: The 'one sample per distribution' assumption is theoretical masochism; in the real world, you can't tell the difference between a shifting distribution and a single outlier having a bad day.
The paper solves the problem of 'choosing a window size' by introducing a 'dynamic adjustment' algorithm that actually just introduces three new, even more confusing settings to tweak.
While the math is 'rigorous,' it provides an elegant solution to a scenario that almost never happens in production, making it a Ferrari parked in a sandbox.
roast.analogy_name: The Perjury of the Pivoting Peephole
roast.analogy_description: Ladies and Gentlemen of the Jury, the defense claims their client can identify a marathon runner's exact location while looking through a telescopic peephole that they are constantly zooming in and out of. They admit they can only see a single sweat bead at any given time. When the Prosecution asks how they can possibly know the runner's trajectory, the defense slams forty pages of calculus on the desk and screams, 'The math says the sweat bead is legally obligated to be near the forehead!' Your Honor, this isn't 'dynamic tracking'; it’s a mathematical hallucination. I move to strike this 'rigorous analysis' from the record—it’s not data science, it's just a very expensive way to guess which way the wind is blowing.

title: Efficient PAC Learning of Halfspaces with Constant Malicious Noise Rate
abstract: Understanding noise tolerance of machine learning algorithms is a central quest in learning theory. In this work, we study the problem of computationally efficient PAC learning of halfspaces in the presence of malicious noise, where an adversary can corrupt both instances and labels of training samples. The best-known noise tolerance either depends on a target error rate under distributional assumptions or on a margin parameter under large-margin conditions. In this work, we show that when both types of conditions are satisfied, it is possible to achieve constant noise tolerance by minimizing a reweighted hinge loss. Our key ingredients include: 1) an efficient algorithm that finds weights to control the gradient deterioration from corrupted samples, and 2) a new analysis on the robustness of the hinge loss equipped with such weights.
author: Jie Shen
aff: Stevens Institute of Technology

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to build a digital filter that can distinguish 'good' data from 'bad' data planted by a hacker trying to break the system. They claim that as long as the data is already perfectly organized and spaced out, they can just turn down the volume on the hacker's nonsense and still get the right answer.
why_bad: Stacked Assumptions: The math only holds up if the data is both perfectly shaped AND widely separated, which is basically like saying you can win a marathon as long as you're allowed to use a motorcycle.
Derivative Methodology: Reweighting a hinge loss is a trick as old as time; the 'new' algorithm feels less like a breakthrough and more like a fresh coat of paint on a 2010 Honda Civic.

The 'Constant' Catch: While they claim to handle a constant noise rate, the fine print suggests the algorithm's performance tanks the moment the adversary stops being 'predictably' malicious and starts being actually clever.
roast.analogy_name: The VIP Club for Exceptionally Orderly People
roast.analogy_description: In a world... where your data is under attack... one team of researchers has a plan. Meet 'The Bouncer.' He’s got one job: keep the trolls out of the nightclub. But there's a twist! The Bouncer only works if every single guest is a 7-foot-tall supermodel standing exactly three feet apart in a perfectly lit hallway. If a troll throws a glitter bomb, The Bouncer just ignores it—but only because he’s already ignored anyone who wasn't a supermodel. It’s a high-stakes thriller where the hero survives the explosion by simply standing in a lead-lined room three miles away. Coming this fall: 'THE REWEIGHTED HINGE LOSS'—rated R for Restricted Distributional Assumptions.

title: Computationally efficient reductions between some statistical models
abstract: We study the problem of approximately transforming a sample from a source statistical model to a sample from a target statistical model without knowing the parameters of the source model, and construct several computationally efficient such reductions between canonical statistical experiments. In particular, we provide computationally efficient procedures that approximately reduce uniform, Erlang, and Laplace location models to general target families. We illustrate our methodology by establishing nonasymptotic reductions between some canonical high-dimensional problems, spanning mixtures of experts, phase retrieval, and signal denoising. Notably, the reductions are structure-preserving and can accommodate missing data. We also point to a possible application in transforming one differentially private mechanism to another.
author: MENGQI LOU;Guy Bresler;Ashwin Pananjady
aff: Georgia Institute of Technology;Massachusetts Institute of Technology;Georgia Institute of Technology
aff_unique_norm: Georgia Institute of Technology;Massachusetts Institute of Technology

roast.laymen_summary: The authors invented a mathematical 'magic trick' to turn one type of data set into another without actually knowing the hidden rules that created the first one. It’s essentially a universal translator for statistics that claims to work even when your data is a total mess or half-missing.
why_bad: The 'computational efficiency' is a theoretical flex that doesn't actually translate to running faster on a normal computer.
It tackles 'high-dimensional problems' that are so abstract they barely exist outside of a whiteboard in a basement.
The jump from 'we can move math around' to 'this fixes privacy' is a leap of faith larger than a crypto bro's belief in Web3.
roast.analogy_description: Picture this: You’ve got a warehouse full of generic lead, and you want it to be gold, but you lost the manual for the lead. Our startup, LeadItGo, provides a 'structure-preserving' funnel that vibrates the lead atoms until they look *almost* like gold to the naked eye. Sure, it only works if the lead is 'Erlang-shaped' and the gold is 'Laplace-flavored,' but we’ve successfully used it to turn a picture of a cat into a blurry spreadsheet. We’re currently pre-revenue, but we’re pivoting to 'Privacy-Preserving Alchemy' because the math is so dense that even the hackers will get bored and leave. We're seeking $50M to solve problems that we'll eventually invent.

title: Quantile Multi-Armed Bandits with 1-bit Feedback
abstract: In this paper, we study a variant of best-arm identification involving elements of risk sensitivity and communication constraints. Specifically, the goal of the learner is to identify the arm with the highest quantile reward, while the communication from an agent (who observes rewards) and the learner (who chooses actions) is restricted to only one bit of feedback per arm pull. We propose an algorithm that utilizes noisy binary search as a subroutine, allowing the learner to estimate quantile rewards through 1-bit feedback. We derive an instance-dependent upper bound on the sample complexity of our algorithm and provide an algorithm-independent lower bound for specific instances, with the two matching to within logarithmic factors under mild conditions, or even to within constant factors in certain low error probability scaling regimes. The lower bound is applicable even in the absence of communication constraints, and thus we conclude that restricting to 1-bit feedback has a minimal impact on the scaling of the sample complexity.
author: Ivan Lau;Jonathan Scarlett
aff: National University of Singapore;National University of Singapore

roast.laymen_summary: We tried to find the best-performing option in a group using only 'yes/no' answers instead of actual data points. We discovered that playing a high-stakes game of 'Hot or Cold' is mathematically almost as fast as having all the numbers, provided you're looking for a specific performance threshold rather than the average.
why_bad: The core 'noisy binary search' mechanism is a well-worn academic antique rebranded as a fresh insight.
The 'mild conditions' required for the math to work are actually so restrictive they essentially assume the answer is already half-known.
The conclusion that 1-bit feedback has a 'minimal impact' ignores the massive practical overhead and computational headache required to achieve that efficiency.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to 'Kitchen Nightmares: Information Theory Edition.' You’re the Head Chef trying to find the soup that is consistently 'just salty enough' at the 90th percentile. The catch? Your sous-chef is forbidden from speaking or showing you the pots; they can only grunt 'too much' or 'not enough' relative to a guess you scream from across the room. The authors are the consultants standing in the corner claiming that this communicative lobotomy doesn't actually slow down the dinner service. They’ve proven that, theoretically, screaming at a mime is just as efficient as having taste buds, provided the kitchen is infinite and the customers never actually want to eat.
roast.tone_check: Scientific accuracy maintained; snark levels optimized.

title: A PAC-Bayesian Link Between Generalisation and Flat Minima
abstract: Modern machine learning usually involves predictors in the overparameterised setting (number of trained parameters greater than dataset size), and their training yields not only good performance on training data, but also good generalisation capacity. This phenomenon challenges many theoretical results, and remains an open problem. To reach a better understanding, we provide novel generalisation bounds involving gradient terms. To do so, we combine the PAC-Bayes toolbox with Poincaré and Log-Sobolev inequalities, avoiding an explicit dependency on the dimension of the predictor space. Our results highlight the positive influence of flat minima (being minima with a neighbourhood nearly minimising the learning problem as well) on generalisation performance, involving directly the benefits of the optimisation phase.
author: Maxime Haddouche;Paul Viallard;Umut Simsekli;Benjamin Guedj
aff: INRIA;Inria;INRIA;University College London, University of London+Inria

roast.laymen_summary: Modern AI models have way more settings than they have data points, which should make them terrible at predicting new things, but they somehow work anyway. This paper tries to prove that models perform better when they settle into a 'broad, flat valley' of solutions rather than a 'narrow, shaky peak' during their training phase.
why_bad: The mathematical bounds are 'vacuous,' meaning the paper 'proves' the error rate is less than 100%, which is about as helpful as a weather app saying there’s a 0-100% chance of rain.
It relies on Log-Sobolev constants that are notoriously impossible to calculate for real-world models, effectively solving a mystery by introducing a bigger mystery.
The core 'discovery'—that flat minima are good—is a well-known concept from the 90s that the authors have simply re-dressed in very expensive, PAC-Bayesian formal wear.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome back to the Pro-Am Data League! Today, our rookie athlete is claiming a 'revolutionary breakthrough' in bowling. Their strategy? Proving—via complex fluid dynamics and gravitational theory—that if you put giant inflatable bumpers in the gutters, you're statistically more likely to hit the pins! The commentators are speechless. They’ve spent twenty pages of math to explain why a wider target is easier to hit than a narrow one, all while ignoring the fact that real pros don't get to use the bumpers. It’s a technical masterpiece of explaining the obvious while completely failing to help anyone actually throw a strike.

title: Full Swap Regret and Discretized Calibration
abstract:
We study the problem of minimizing swap regret in structured normal-form games. Players have a very large (potentially infinite) number of pure actions, but each action has an embedding into $d$-dimensional space and payoffs are given by bilinear functions of these embeddings. We provide an efficient learning algorithm for this setting that incurs at most $\tilde{O}(T^{(d+1)/(d+3)})$ swap regret after $T$ rounds.

To achieve this, we introduce a new online learning problem we call \emph{full swap regret minimization}. In this problem, a learner repeatedly takes a (randomized) action in a bounded convex $d$-dimensional action set $\mathcal{K}$ and then receives a loss from the adversary, with the goal of minimizing their regret with respect to the \emph{worst-case} swap function mapping $\mathcal{K}$ to $\mathcal{K}$. For varied assumptions about the convexity and smoothness of the loss functions, we design algorithms with full swap regret bounds ranging from $O(T^{d/(d+2)})$ to $O(T^{(d+1)/(d+2)})$.

Finally, we apply these tools to the problem of online forecasting to minimize calibration error, showing that several notions of calibration can be viewed as specific instances of full swap regret. In particular, we design efficient algorithms for online forecasting that guarantee at most $O(T^{1/3})$ $\ell_2$-calibration error and $O(\max(\sqrt{\epsilon T}, T^{1/3}))$ \emph{discretized-calibration} error (when the forecaster is restricted to predicting multiples of $\epsilon$).
author: Maxwell Fishelson;Robert Kleinberg;Princewill Okoroafor;Renato Paes Leme;Jon Schneider;Yifeng Teng
aff: Massachusetts Institute of Technology;Google+Cornell University;Department of Computer Science, Cornell University;Google;Google;Google Research

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to build a math-heavy shortcut for AI to avoid 'buyer's remorse' in games where they have an infinite number of choices. While they claim to have found a way to stop the AI from wishing it had picked a different move, the solution becomes catastrophically slow as soon as the choices get even slightly more complex.
why_bad: The 'Curse of Dimensionality' makes the regret bounds explode; as soon as your actions have more than a couple of features, the algorithm learns slower than a dial-up modem.
The authors claim the algorithm is 'efficient,' but the computational cost of tracking every possible alternative action in a continuous space is a nightmare for actual hardware.
The leap from 'swap regret' to 'calibration' feels like a theoretical stretch that adds complexity without actually outperforming existing, simpler forecasting methods.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Infinite Second-Guesser
roast.analogy_description: Counsel, the defendant claims they can play a game with infinite moves without ever wishing they’d done something else. But look at the evidence! To avoid 'regret,' they’ve built a machine that considers every possible alternative universe simultaneously. The prosecution argues that a learning rate this slow isn't an 'algorithm'—it’s a filibuster. Your Honor, you cannot claim to be 'efficient' when your math takes longer to settle a bet than it takes for the sun to burn out. We move to strike these $T^{d/d+something}$ bounds from the record as being 'mathematically interesting but practically inadmissible' in any real-world court of AI.

title: How rotation invariant algorithms are fooled by noise on sparse targets
abstract:
It is well known that rotation invariant algorithms are sub-optimal for learning sparse linear problems,
when the number of examples is below the input dimension. This includes any gradient descent
trained neural net with a fully-connected input layer initialized with a rotationally symmetric
distribution. The simplest sparse problem is learning a single feature out of d features. In that case
the classification error or regression loss of rotation invariant algorithms grows with 1−n/d, where n
is the number of examples seen. These lower bounds become vacuous when the number of examples
n reaches the dimension d. After d examples, the gradient space has full rank and any weight vector
can be expressed, including the unit vector that determines the target feature.
In this work, we show that when noise is added to this sparse linear problem, rotation invariant
algorithms are still sub-optimal after seeing d or more examples. We prove this via a lower bound
for the Bayes optimal algorithm on a rotationally symmetrized problem. We then prove much
lower upper bounds on the same problem for a large variety of algorithms that are non-invariant by
rotations.
Finally, we analyze the gradient flow trajectories of many standard optimization algorithms
(such as AdaGrad) on the same noisy feature learning problem, and show how they veer away from
the noisy sparse targets. We then contrast them with a group of non-rotation invariant algorithms
that veer towards the sparse targets.
We believe that the lower bounds method and trajectory categorization will be crucial for
analyzing other families of algorithms with different classes of invariances.
author: Manfred K Warmuth;Wojciech Kotlowski;Matt Jones;Ehsan Amid
aff: Google Research;Poznan University of Technology;University of Colorado Boulder;Google DeepMind
aff_unique_norm: Google;Poznan University of Technology;University of Colorado

roast.laymen_summary: Most basic AI models are like kids who try to solve a 'Where's Waldo' page by looking at the whole book at once; they get so overwhelmed by the background noise that they miss the obvious guy in the stripes. This paper proves that even when you give these models extra data, if they don't have a 'hunch' about where to look, they'll end up chasing random shadows instead of the actual target.
why_bad: The 'Single Feature' Problem: Using a toy model with only one active variable is like trying to explain the entire ocean by studying a single goldfish in a bowl; it’s too simplistic for the claims made.
The 'We Been Knew' Factor: Proving that rotationally symmetric models struggle with sparse data is a bit like publishing a paper titled 'Water is Wet: Even When You Add Salt.'

Theoretical Vacuum: The analysis of gradient flow trajectories ignores the fact that real-world engineers use tricks (like regularization) specifically to prevent the exact 'veering away' the authors spent 20 pages proving.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to DisruptiveSeek, the only search-and-rescue startup that values 'fairness' over finding people! Our proprietary method for finding your lost keys in a park involves spinning our searchers at 60 RPM until they have no idea which way is North—this ensures they are 'rotationally invariant' and aren't biased toward any particular patch of grass. While a normal person (a non-invariant algorithm) would just look at the ground, our team treats the sky, the trees, and the local Taco Bell with equal importance. When a squirrel runs by (that’s the noise), our searchers don't just get distracted; they actually start sprinting toward the horizon because they've mathematically convinced themselves the squirrel is your keychain. We're seeking $5M in seed funding to prove that being completely lost is actually a rigorous statistical property.

title: Information-Theoretic Guarantees for Recovering Low-Rank Tensors from Symmetric Rank-One Measurements
abstract: We investigate the sample complexity of recovering tensors with low symmetric rank from symmetric rank-one measurements, a setting particularly motivated by the study of higher-order interactions in statistics and the analysis of two-layer polynomial neural networks. Using a covering number argument, we analyze the performance of the symmetric rank minimization program and establish near-optimal sample complexity bounds when the underlying distribution is log-concave. Our measurement model involves random symmetric rank-one tensors, leading to involved probability calculations. To address these challenges, we employ the Carbery-Wright inequality, a powerful tool for studying anti-concentration properties of random polynomials, and leverage orthogonal polynomial expansions. Additionally, we provide a sample complexity lower bound via Fano’s inequality, and discuss broader implications of our results for two-layer polynomial networks.
author: Eren C. Kizildag
aff: University of Illinois, Urbana Champaign

roast.laymen_summary: This paper attempts to calculate the absolute minimum number of simple, one-dimensional snapshots required to perfectly reconstruct a complex, multi-dimensional data structure. It’s essentially trying to prove exactly how many times you need to glance at a rotating object to memorize its entire shape without making a single mistake.
why_bad: The 'Spherical Cow' Assumption: The authors rely on 'log-concave' distributions, which is academic-speak for 'this only works if the data is perfectly behaved,' a scenario that rarely survives first contact with real-world noise.
Loose Ends in the Bounds: While they claim 'near-optimal' results, the gap between their theory and a practical lower bound is wide enough to drive a GPU cluster through.

Purely Hallucinated Application: They claim this helps understand neural networks, but without a single line of code or empirical test, it's like writing a manual on how to fly a plane based entirely on bird-watching.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to 'Chef’s Theorem,' where today our contestants are trying to reconstruct a five-tier, 3D wedding cake... by looking only at the grease stains left on a single flat napkin. Our authors are using the 'Symmetric Rank-One' technique, which is like trying to guess the flavor of the entire cake by licking one stray sprinkle. They’ve brought out some incredibly fancy French kitchen gadgets like the 'Carbery-Wright' spatula to prove their math is solid, but there's a catch: they've assumed the cake is a perfectly symmetrical loaf of Jell-O. In the real world, cakes have crumbs, layers, and structural collapses, making their elegant 'log-concave' recipe about as useful as a chocolate teapot in a real kitchen. It's a beautiful lecture on the physics of flour, but nobody is actually getting dessert.

title: An Online Feasible Point Method for Benign Generalized Nash Equilibrium Problems.
abstract:
We consider a repeatedly played generalized Nash equilibrium game. This induces a multi-agent online learning problem with joint constraints. An important challenge in this setting is that the feasible set for each agent depends on the simultaneous moves of the other agents and, therefore, varies over time. As a consequence, the agents face time-varying constraints, which are not adversarial but rather endogenous to the system. Prior work in this setting focused on convergence to a feasible solution in the limit via integrating the constraints in the objective as a penalty function.  However, no existing work can guarantee that the constraints are satisfied for all iterations while simultaneously guaranteeing convergence to a generalized Nash equilibrium. This is a problem of fundamental theoretical interest and practical relevance.
In this work, we introduce a new online feasible point method. Under the assumption that limited communication between the agents is allowed, this method guarantees feasibility. We identify the class of benign generalized Nash equilibrium problems, for which the convergence of our method to the equilibrium is guaranteed. We set this class of benign generalized Nash equilibrium games in context with existing definitions and illustrate our method with examples.
author: Sarah Sachs;Hedi Hadiji;Tim van Erven;Mathias Staudigl
aff: Bocconi University;CentraleSupelec;University of Amsterdam;Universität Mannheim+Maastricht University
aff_unique_norm: Bocconi University;CentraleSupélec;University of Amsterdam;University of Mannheim;Maastricht University

roast.laymen_summary: In a game where everyone's actions constantly change the rules for everyone else, these authors tried to ensure no one ever breaks a rule even for a split second. Their solution requires everyone to pinky-swear and share secrets, and it only works if the game is 'benign'—which is academic speak for 'unrealistically easy.'
why_bad: The definition of 'benign' is suspiciously narrow, essentially moving the goalposts to a field where it's impossible to miss.
Requiring 'limited communication' undermines the whole point of a decentralized Nash equilibrium, making it less of a game and more of a choreographed dance.
The method prioritizes theoretical feasibility over practical performance, likely resulting in agents that are so cautious they never actually achieve anything useful.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to 'Top Chef: Extreme Constraints.' You and three other chefs are sharing one tiny stove, and the height of the flame depends on how many onions your neighbor is chopping. Usually, chefs just hope the kitchen doesn't burn down before the judges arrive. These authors claim to have a way to keep the kitchen perfectly safe every single second. The catch? You all have to constantly whisper your moves to each other, and the recipe only works if you're making 'benign' toast. It’s a flawless system for a meal nobody wants to eat, in a kitchen that functions more like a telepathic cult than a workspace.

title: A Complete Characterization of Learnability for Stochastic Noisy Bandits
abstract:
We study the stochastic noisy bandit problem with an unknown reward function $f^*$ in a known function class $\mathcal{F}$. Formally, a model $M$ maps arms $\pi$ to a probability distribution $M(\pi)$ of reward. A model class $\mathcal{M}$ is a collection of models. For each model $M$, define its mean reward function $f^M(\pi)=\mathbb{E}_{r \sim M(\pi)}[r]$.  In the bandit learning problem, we proceed in rounds, pulling one arm $\pi$ each round and observing a reward sampled from $M(\pi)$. With knowledge of $\mathcal{M}$, supposing that the true model $M\in \mathcal{M}$, the objective is to identify an arm $\hat{\pi}$ of near-maximal mean reward $f^M(\hat{\pi})$ with high probability in a bounded number of rounds. If this is possible, then the model class is said to be learnable. 

Importantly, a result of Hanneke and Yang (2023)  shows there exist model classes for which learnability is undecidable. However, the model class they consider features deterministic rewards, and they raise the question of whether learnability is decidable for classes containing sufficiently noisy models. More formally, for any function class $\mathcal{F}$ of mean reward functions, we denote by $\mathcal{M}_{\mathcal{F}}$ the set of all models $M$ such that $f^M \in \mathcal{F}$. In other words, $\mathcal{M} _{\mathcal{F}}$ admits arbitrary zero-mean noise. Hanneke and Yang (2023)  ask the question: Can one give a simple complete characterization of which function classes $\mathcal{F}$ satisfy that $\mathcal{M} _{\mathcal{F}}$ is learnable?

For the first time, we answer this question in the positive by giving a complete characterization of learnability for model classes $\mathcal{M}_{\mathcal{F}}$. In addition to that, we also describe the full spectrum of possible optimal query complexities. Further, we prove adaptivity is sometimes necessary to achieve the optimal query complexity. Last, we revisit an important complexity measure for interactive decision making, the Decision-Estimation-Coefficient (Foster et al., 2021, 2023),  and propose a new variant of the DEC which also characterizes learnability in this setting.
author: Steve Hanneke;Kun Wang
aff: Purdue University;Purdue University

roast.laymen_summary: This paper tries to figure out if you can actually 'solve' a game where every move you make gives you a result buried under random static. The authors claim to have found the ultimate rulebook for deciding which of these noisy games are winnable and which are just a mathematical waste of time.
why_bad: The 'complete characterization' relies on a noise assumption so specific that it bypasses the truly difficult edge cases identified by previous researchers.
The proposed 'new variant' of the Decision-Estimation-Coefficient (DEC) provides more of a cosmetic rebranding than a fundamental theoretical breakthrough.
The proof for necessary adaptivity is essentially a complex way of stating that you can't find a needle in a haystack if you aren't allowed to move the hay.
roast.analogy_name: The Gaslighting Casino: Static in the Attic
roast.analogy_description: In a world where every slot machine is actively gaslighting you, one team of researchers claims they've found the 'God Equation' for winning. They argue that as long as the machines are screaming loud enough, the truth is hidden in the noise. But the critics are walking out of the theater early—because it turns out the 'God Equation' only works if you already know how loud the screaming is. Coming this summer: a high-stakes thriller where the math is 'complete,' the results are 'stochastic,' and the peer reviewers have absolutely zero chill. *The Bandit: Noise Always Wins.*
roast.real_talk_score: High-tier theory that forgot to check if anyone actually needed another flavor of DEC.

title: Fast Convergence of $\Phi$-Divergence Along the Unadjusted Langevin Algorithm and Proximal Sampler
abstract: We study the mixing time of two popular discrete-time Markov chains in continuous space, the Unadjusted Langevin Algorithm and the Proximal Sampler, which are discretizations of the Langevin dynamics. We extend mixing time analyses for these Markov chains to hold in $\Phi$-divergence. We show that any $\Phi$-divergence arising from a twice-differentiable strictly convex function $\Phi$ converges to $0$ exponentially fast along these Markov chains, under the assumption that their stationary distributions satisfy the corresponding $\Phi$-Sobolev inequality, which holds for example when the target distribution of the Langevin dynamics is strongly log-concave. Our setting includes as special cases popular mixing time regimes, namely the mixing in chi-squared divergence under a Poincaré inequality, and the mixing in relative entropy under a log-Sobolev inequality. Our results follow by viewing the sampling algorithms as noisy channels and bounding the contraction coefficients arising in the appropriate strong data processing inequalities.
author: Siddharth Mitra;Andre Wibisono
aff: Yale University;Yale University

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to prove that two common AI sampling methods find their target quickly by measuring their progress with a fancy, all-encompassing math yardstick. Unfortunately, their 'breakthrough' only works if the target is a perfectly shaped, easy-to-find hill, which is the equivalent of bragging that you can find your own front door in broad daylight.
why_bad: The 'Generalization Trap': They essentially rebranded existing proofs for specific cases into a more abstract framework without actually discovering any new physical behavior.
The 'Strong Log-Concave' Crutch: The proofs rely on the assumption that the data is perfectly behaved, a luxury rarely afforded by real-world datasets that are messy and complex.
Lack of Algorithmic Innovation: It uses high-level information theory to describe existing algorithms but fails to provide a single reason why a practitioner should actually change their code.
roast.analogy_name: The Olympic Gold Medal for Downhill Walking
roast.analogy_description: Welcome back to the 'Theoretical Physics Open,' where our athletes are currently celebrating a 'massive victory.' Look at that form! They’ve just mathematically proven that if you stand at the top of a perfectly smooth, frictionless slide, you will—get this—eventually reach the bottom. Stunning! The commentators are speechless because they're using a 'Generalized Gravity Metric' to prove what we already knew from 'Standard Gravity.' It’s a bold strategy to ignore the fact that the actual mountains researchers have to climb are covered in jagged rocks, sudden cliffs, and localized gravity wells. They’ve built a state-of-the-art GPS for a straight line.

title: On Generalization Bounds for Neural Networks with Low Rank Layers
abstract: While previous optimization results have suggested that deep neural networks tend to favor low-rank weight matrices, the implications of this inductive bias on generalization bounds remain underexplored. In this paper, we apply a chain rule for Gaussian complexity (Maurer, 2016) to analyze how low-rank layers in deep networks can prevent the accumulation of rank and dimensionality factors that typically multiply across layers. This approach yields generalization bounds for rank and spectral norm constrained networks. We compare our results to prior generalization bounds for deep networks, highlighting how deep networks with low-rank layers can achieve better generalization than those with full-rank layers. Additionally, we discuss how this framework provides new perspectives on the generalization capabilities of deep networks exhibiting neural collapse.
author: Andrea Pinto;Akshay Rangamani;Tomaso A Poggio
aff: ;New Jersey Institute of Technology;

roast.laymen_summary: The authors are trying to prove that AI models get better grades when they're forced to use 'simplified' logic instead of overcomplicating every single thought. By mathematically showing that 'less is more' in the brain of the network, they argue we can finally predict why these models don't just hallucinate wildly when they see something new.
why_bad: The 'Maurer-Special' Microwave Recipe: The paper basically just reheats a 2016 math trick and sprinkles some 2024 buzzwords on it without adding any actual new ingredients.
The Vacuous Bound Problem: The mathematical 'guarantees' provided are so loose that they’re about as helpful as a weather forecast predicting 'between zero and one hundred inches of rain.'

The Spherical Cow Assumption: It assumes real-world neural networks are perfectly 'low-rank' (organized and efficient), which is like assuming a frat house is 'perfectly clean' because you only looked at the ceiling.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome back to 'Kitchen Kernels,' where today's chef tried to win the grand prize by serving a single bouillon cube in a bowl of lukewarm water. He calls it 'Low-Rank Consommé.' His argument? By not 'over-complicating' the dish with actual vegetables, meat, or seasoning, the guest’s palate won't be overwhelmed by complexity. He even brought a 2016 textbook to prove that, theoretically, this cube contains the essence of a whole cow. But when the judges took a sip, they realized that while he technically avoided the 'clutter' of a real soup, he also failed to provide any actual nutrition. It’s a mathematically 'pure' dish that leaves everyone at the table starving.

title: Clustering with bandit feedback: breaking down the computation/information gap
abstract:
We investigate the Clustering with Bandit feedback Problem (CBP). A learner interacts with an $N$-armed stochastic bandit with $d$-dimensional subGaussian feedback. There exists a hidden partition of the arms into $K$ groups, such that arms within the same group, share the same mean vector. The learner's task is to uncover this hidden partition with the smallest budget - i.e. the least number of observation - and with a probability of error smaller than a prescribed constant $\delta$. In this paper, (i) we derive a non asymptotic lower bound for the budget, and (ii) we introduce the computationally efficient ACB algorithm, whose budget  matches the lower bound in most regimes. 
We improve on the performance of a uniform sampling strategy. Importantly, contrary to the batch setting, we establish that there is no computation-information gap in the bandit setting.
author: Victor Thuot;Alexandra Carpentier;Christophe Giraud;Nicolas Verzelen
aff: ;Universität Potsdam;Université Paris Saclay;INRAE

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to group a bunch of mystery slot machines into secret 'teams' by playing them as few times as possible. They claimed to find a shortcut that makes this grouping process perfectly efficient, supposedly proving that being smart doesn't require extra effort in this specific game.
why_bad: The 'no computation-information gap' claim is a massive stretch; reviewers felt the authors ignored the hardest versions of the problem to make their math look prettier.
The algorithm's performance only matches the theoretical limit in 'most regimes,' which is academic speak for 'it breaks the moment things get actually interesting or difficult.'
The comparison to the 'batch setting' is apples-to-oranges, making their main contribution feel more like a technicality than a breakthrough.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome back to the Intelligence Open, where the authors are trying to sort 100 mystery appetizers into 'Taco Tuesday' or 'Nuclear Waste' using nothing but a single toothpick! They’re screaming from the sidelines that they’ve found the 'Perfect Poke,' claiming they can sort the whole buffet faster than anyone in history. But wait—the refs are looking at the replay! It turns out the authors only tested their strategy on trays where the tacos were literally glowing and the waste was frozen solid. You can't claim you've mastered the 'Information Gap' when you're only playing on the 'Easy' difficulty setting. That’s a flag on the play for over-hyping a standard move, and the paper is headed straight to the locker room!

title: For Universal Multiclass Online Learning, Bandit Feedback and Full Supervision are Equivalent
abstract:
We study the problem of multiclass online learning under $\textit{bandit feedback}$ within the framework of $\textit{universal learning}$  [Bousquet, Hanneke, Moran, van Handel, and Yehudayoff; STOC '21].
    
In multiclass online learning under bandit feedback, it is well known that no concept class $\mathcal{C}$ is $\textit{uniformly}$ learnable when the effective label space is unbounded, or in other words, no online learner guarantees a finite bound on the expected number of mistakes holding uniformly over all realizable data sequences. In contrast, surprisingly, we show that in the case of $\textit{universal}$ learnability of concept classes $\mathcal{C}$, there is an exact equivalence between multiclass online learnability under bandit feedback and full supervision, in both the realizable and agnostic settings.
    
More specifically, our first main contribution is a theory that establishes an inherent dichotomy in multiclass online learning under bandit feedback within the realizable setting. In particular, for any concept class $\mathcal{C}$ even when the effective label space is unbounded, we have: (1) If $\mathcal{C}$ does not have an infinite multiclass Littlestone tree, then there is a deterministic online learner that makes only finitely many mistakes against any realizable adversary, crucially without placing a uniform bound on the number of mistakes. (2) If $\mathcal{C}$ has an infinite multiclass Littlestone tree, then there is a strategy for the realizable adversary that forces any learner, including randomized, to make linear expected number of mistakes. Furthermore, our second main contribution reveals a similar trend in the agnostic setting.
author: Steve Hanneke;Amirreza Shaeiri;Hongao Wang
aff: Purdue University;Purdue University;Purdue University

roast.laymen_summary: In the world of machine learning, getting a 'wrong' grade is usually much harder to learn from than being told the correct answer, but these authors claim that for certain complex problems, both methods are actually identical. They argue that as long as you have an infinite amount of time to fail, not knowing the right answer is just as 'easy' as knowing it, which is mathematically elegant and practically useless.
why_bad: The 'finite but unbounded' mistake bound is a mathematical fairy tale; it means the algorithm could fail for a trillion years and still technically be 'successful.'
The core proof relies so heavily on existing 2021 framework that the 'novelty' feels less like a breakthrough and more like a very long footnote.
The jump to the 'agnostic setting' is more of a speculative leap of faith than a rigorous structural proof, leaving reviewers unconvinced by the hand-waving.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to 'Kitchen Nightmares: Infinity Edition.' In one corner, you have a chef who tells you exactly which spice you forgot. In the other, you have a judge who just spits on the floor every time your dish isn't perfect. The authors are arguing that these two scenarios are exactly the same because, given an infinite supply of eggs and a literal eternity, you will eventually stumble upon the correct omelet in both kitchens. It’s a bold take that ignores the fact that the restaurant has burned down, the guests are dead, and 'eventually' is doing enough heavy lifting to snap a titanium barbell. Scientifically accurate? Sure. Helpful for dinner? Absolutely not.

title: Generalisation under gradient descent via deterministic PAC-Bayes
abstract: We establish disintegrated PAC-Bayesian generalisation bounds for models trained with gradient descent methods or continuous gradient flows. Contrary to standard practice in the PAC-Bayesian setting, our result applies to optimisation algorithms that are deterministic, without requiring any de-randomisation step. Our bounds are fully computable, depending on the density of the initial distribution and the Hessian of the training objective over the trajectory. We show that our framework can be applied to a variety of iterative optimisation algorithms, including stochastic gradient descent (SGD), momentum-based schemes, and damped Hamiltonian dynamics.
author: Eugenio Clerico;Tyler Farghly;George Deligiannidis;Benjamin Guedj;Arnaud Doucet
aff: Universitat Pompeu Fabra;University of Oxford;Oxford, University of Oxford;University College London, University of London+Inria;
aff_unique_norm: Universitat Pompeu Fabra;University of Oxford;University College London;INRIA;

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to create a mathematical 'safety rating' to prove that AI models will actually work on new data, specifically for models that don't rely on random luck during training. They claim they can predict an AI's success just by measuring how 'curvy' the math landscape was while the model was learning its way to the top.
why_bad: The 'computable' bounds are computationally ruinous, requiring the calculation of second-order derivatives (Hessians) across the entire training path, which would make a supercomputer cry.

The resulting error bounds are likely 'vacuous,' meaning the math technically works but tells you the error rate is 'somewhere between 0% and 10,000%,' which is about as helpful as a weather report predicting 'weather.'
It relies on the assumption of a 'disintegrated' density that simplifies the math but ignores the chaotic, non-smooth reality of how actual deep learning models behave in the wild.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a startup pitching a revolutionary GPS that guarantees you won't get lost, but there's a catch: it only works if you stop every six inches to measure the microscopic curvature of the pavement with a laser. We’re talking about 'The Deterministic Commute'—a system that proves you'll reach the office not by looking at a map, but by calculating the exact vibration of your tires over every single pebble you hit. It’s technically 'fully computable' if you have ten million years to spare, but by the time you finish the math for your trip to Starbucks, the sun has gone supernova. It’s a brilliant solution for a driver who doesn't exist, traveling on a road that’s too smooth to be real, providing a safety guarantee that's essentially: 'You will arrive, or you won't.'

title: Differentially Private Multi-Sampling from Distributions
abstract:
Many algorithms have been developed to estimate probability distributions subject to differential privacy
(DP): such an algorithm takes as input independent samples from a distribution and estimates the density
function in a way that is insensitive to any one sample. A recent line of work, initiated by Raskhodnikova
et al. (Neurips ’21), explores a weaker objective: a differentially private algorithm that approximates
a single sample from the distribution. Raskhodnikova et al. studied the sample complexity of DP
single-sampling i.e., the minimum number of samples needed to perform this task. They showed that the
sample complexity of DP single-sampling is less than the sample complexity of DP learning for certain
distribution classes. We define two variants of multi-sampling, where the goal is to privately approximate
$m > 1$ samples. This better models the realistic scenario where synthetic data is needed for exploratory
data analysis.

A baseline solution to multi-sampling is to invoke a single-sampling algorithm $m$ times on independently drawn datasets of samples. When the data comes from a finite domain, we improve over the baseline by a factor of $m$ in the sample complexity. When the data comes from a Gaussian, Ghazi et al. (Neurips ’23) show that single-sampling can be performed under approximate differential privacy; we
show it is possible to single- and multi-sample Gaussians with known covariance subject to pure DP. Our
solution uses a variant of the Laplace mechanism that is of independent interest.

We also give sample complexity lower bounds, one for strong multi-sampling of finite distributions
and another for weak multi-sampling of bounded-covariance Gaussians.
author: Albert Cheu;Debanuj Nayak
aff: Google;Boston University

roast.laymen_summary: The authors are trying to generate 'fake but realistic' data samples from a secret source without accidentally snitching on any individual person's private information. They claim to have found a shortcut to generate a whole batch of these decoys at once, rather than just one at a time, but mostly for very simple types of data.
why_bad: The 'innovation' is largely incremental, taking existing techniques for single-sample privacy and just scaling them up to 'm' samples without offering a massive conceptual leap.
The Gaussian results rely on 'known covariance,' which is the research equivalent of saying you can predict the weather as long as someone tells you exactly what the clouds are doing first.
The theoretical bounds for finite distributions are mathematically tidy but practically underwhelming for anyone dealing with real-world data that doesn't fit into a neat, small box.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Anonymous Appetizers
roast.analogy_description: Picture a courtroom where the Defense claims they can cater a gala using a 'secret family recipe' without anyone figuring out which cousin provided the secret ingredient. The Prosecution (the reviewers) is unimpressed. 'Your Honor,' they argue, 'the Defense isn't inventing a new recipe; they're just handing out five crackers instead of one and calling it a multi-course meal!' The Defense argues their 'Multi-Sampling' technique is revolutionary, but the Judge notes they only know how to serve crackers (finite domains) or plain soup (Gaussians) where they already know the salt content (known covariance). It’s not a breakthrough in culinary privacy; it’s just an efficient way to plate the same bland snacks while ignoring the fact that the Chef already leaked the main ingredients to the bailiff.

title: A Characterization of List Regression
abstract:
There has been a recent interest in understanding and characterizing the sample complexity of list learning tasks, where the learning algorithm is allowed to make a short list of $k$ predictions, and we simply require one of the predictions to be correct. This includes recent works characterizing the PAC sample complexity of standard list classification and online list classification.

Adding to this theme, in this work, we provide a complete characterization of list PAC _regression_. We propose two combinatorial dimensions, namely the $k$-OIG dimension and the $k$-fat-shattering dimension, and show that they characterize realizable and agnostic $k$-list regression respectively. These quantities generalize known dimensions for standard regression. Our work thus extends existing list learning characterizations from classification to regression.
author: Chirag Pabbaraju;Sahasrajit Sarmasarkar
aff: Stanford University;Stanford University

roast.laymen_summary: Usually, AI tries to predict a single exact number, but this paper studies what happens when we let the AI hedge its bets with a 'top-five' list of guesses. The authors invented two new mathematical yardsticks to calculate how much data is needed to make sure at least one of those guesses isn't total garbage.
why_bad: Incremental Novelty: The paper essentially takes existing 'list learning' logic for categories and just swaps in numbers, making it feel more like a predictable sequel than a breakthrough.
Theoretical Overkill: It introduces the 'k-fat-shattering dimension,' a term that sounds like a hardcore gym injury and provides about as much practical utility for real-world engineers.
Low Utility: In the real world, if your regression model needs five chances to get a number right, you don't need a new mathematical characterization—you need a better model.
roast.analogy_description: In a world where being wrong is a firing offense... one group of mathematicians decided to change the rules. Coming this summer: THE LIST. Follow a rogue forecaster who refuses to say it’s 75 degrees, instead insisting it is either 40, 75, 92, or possibly 11. Watch in awe as they invent the 'Fat-Shattering Dimension'—the most intense-sounding math that basically just means 'I'm throwing five darts at the board and calling it a bullseye.' It’s a high-stakes thriller where the hero doesn't find the truth; they just narrow down the lies. Rated R for 'Redundant Mathematical Extensions.'

title: Sharp bounds on aggregate expert error
abstract: We revisit the classic problem of aggregating binary advice from conditionally independent experts, also known as the Naive Bayes setting. Our quantity of interest is the error probability of the optimal decision rule. In the case of symmetric errors (sensitivity = specificity), reasonably tight bounds on the optimal error probability are known. In the general asymmetric case, we are not aware of any nontrivial estimates on this quantity. Our contribution consists of sharp upper and lower bounds on the optimal error probability in the general case, which recover and sharpen the best known results in the symmetric special case. Since this turns out to be closely connected to bounding the total variation distance between two product distributions, our results also have bearing on this important and challenging problem.
author: Aryeh Kontorovich;Ariel Avital
aff: Ben Gurion University of the Negev;Ben Gurion University of the Negev, Technion
aff_unique_norm: Ben Gurion University of the Negev

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers attempted to calculate the exact mathematical limit on how often a group of 'experts' will get an answer wrong, specifically when they are better at confirming truths than spotting lies. It’s essentially a heavy-duty statistical audit of how much two different sets of probabilities overlap when you combine multiple guesses.
why_bad: Incremental Theory: The paper refines a classic problem (Naive Bayes) that most practitioners consider 'good enough,' making the new bounds feel like putting premium racing tires on a 2004 Toyota Camry.
Lack of Empirical Receipts: Reviewers likely balked at the 'all-math, no-math-application' approach, as the authors failed to show how these 'sharp' bounds actually improve real AI performance.
Niche Impact: While the connection to 'total variation distance' is mathematically elegant, it’s seen as a theoretical flex rather than a solution to a pressing bottleneck in modern machine learning.
roast.analogy_name: The Perjury of the Pessimistic Panelists
roast.analogy_description: Picture a high-stakes courtroom drama where you’ve hired ten 'experts' to decide if a defendant stole a bagel. In the old days, we assumed these experts were equally likely to wrongly convict as they were to wrongly acquit. But in reality, Expert A is a cynical grump who thinks everyone is a thief, and Expert B is a pushover who wouldn't convict a golden retriever. This paper is a 40-page legal manual trying to calculate the exact, absolute probability that this motley crew will reach a bogus verdict. The judge—our reviewer—tossed the case out because the court is already backlogged, the bagel is eaten, and nobody needs a proof-heavy dissertation to confirm that a biased jury is a statistical nightmare.

title: On the Hardness of Learning One Hidden Layer Neural Networks
abstract: In this work, we consider the problem of learning one hidden layer ReLU neural networks with inputs from $\mathbb{R}^d$. We show that this learning problem is hard under standard cryptographic assumptions even when: (1) the size of the neural network is polynomial in $d$, (2) its input distribution is a standard Gaussian, and (3) the noise is Gaussian and polynomially small in $d$. Our hardness result is based on the hardness of the Continuous Learning with Errors (CLWE) problem, and in particular, is based on the largely believed worst-case hardness of approximately solving the shortest vector problem up to a multiplicative polynomial factor.
author: Shuchen Li;Ilias Zadik;Manolis Zampetakis
aff: Yale University;Yale University;Yale University

roast.laymen_summary: This paper mathematically proves that training even a tiny, single-layer AI brain is so difficult that if you could do it easily, you'd be able to crack high-level military encryption. It turns out that even with 'clean' data and minimal noise, the math behind a simple neural network is a locked vault that we still haven't found the key to.
why_bad: The paper proves the problem is 'hard' in theory, but completely ignores the fact that millions of practitioners are successfully training these networks every day in the real world.
The reliance on the 'Continuous Learning with Errors' (CLWE) framework is seen as a bit of a theoretical crutch that doesn't provide new insights into actual AI behavior.
Reviewers noted that the 'worst-case' scenario focus makes the result practically irrelevant for anyone not trying to build a career in cryptography or abstract geometry.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome back to the Mathematical Olympics! Here we have the authors trying to run a simple 40-yard dash, but they’ve decided to prove that the track is actually a 500-dimensional maze constructed of invisible lasers. They’re telling the fans that unless you can solve a Rubik's cube while blindfolded and sprinting, you'll never reach the finish line. The crowd is confused because they just watched a bunch of teenagers finish the race in five seconds using 'Stupid Gradient Descent,' but the authors are insisting those teenagers don't exist because the math says they should have tripped over a polynomial factor by now. It’s a bold strategy to tell the audience the sport is impossible while the game is literally happening behind them!
